coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of N doped graphene quantum dots-interspersed CdWO4 heterostructure nanorods as an effective photocatalyst with enhanced photoelectrochemical performance
coredata.dc:creator: Pei, Yu
Fan, Tao
Chu, Hang
Ge, Yuancai
Yang, Yang
Dong, Pei
Baines, Robert
Ye, Mingxin
Shen, Jianfeng
coredata.dc:description: Herein, N doped graphene quantum dots-interspersed Cadmium tungstate (CdWO4) nanorods (N-GQDs-CdWO4) were synthesized via decorating the N-GQDs on the surface of CdWO4 nanorods in three steps. The morphological and structural properties of the N-GQDs-CdWO4 nanorods were thoroughly investigated by X-ray diffraction spectroscopy, field-emission scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, and ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy. Based on imaging techniques, it became clear that the N-GQDs decorated on the surface of CdWO4 nanorods formed a heterostructure. This nanocomposite exhibits enhanced photoelectrochemical performances—more than four times those of bare CdWO4. In addition, the nanocomposite's photocatalytic activity under ultra-violet light irradiation was almost six fold that of bare CdWO4. We studied the mechanism governing its exceptional performance. It indicates that the existence of a heterostructure induces a synergistic effect between the N-GQDs and CdWO4 nanorods that bolsters photoelectric performance. Our work provides a feasible method to synthesize 0D/3D nanocomposites with enhanced photoelectrochemical performances, and offers a solution to rectify current solar energy shortfalls.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N doped graphene quantum dots
CdWO4 nanorods
Hydrothermal method
Photocatalyst

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterizations of N-GQDs/CdWO4 nanorods
Photocatalytic and enhancement mechanism of N-GQDs/CdWO4 nanorods

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the hydrothermal preparation method for N-GQDs/CdWO4 Nanorods. (b) XRD patterns of the bare CdWO4 and N-GQDs/CdWO4 nanocomposites.

Characterization of N-GQDs/CdWO4 nanorods. (a, b) SEM images of N-GQDs/CdWO4 nanorods. (c, d, e, f) Elemental mapping of C, O, N, Cd, and W elements in N-GQDs-CdWO4-2. (h, i) TEM and HRTEM images of N-GQDs/CdWO4 nanorods.
(a) XPS survey spectra and (b) peak deconvolution of C 1s region of N-GQDs-CdWO4-2 composite.
Raman spectra of CdWO4, N-GQDs and N-GQDs-CdWO4-2 samples.

The photoelectrochemical performance of bare CdWO4 electrode in Na2SO4 solution under Hg light (a) linear sweep voltammetry curves of bare CdWO4 electrode with and without UV–visible light illumination (b) Photocurrent density–time curves of bare CdWO4 electrode.

The photoelectrochemical performance of bare CdWO4 electrode in Na2SO4 solution under Hg light (a) EIS Nyquist plots of bare CdWO4 and N-GQDs/CdWO4-1(2, 3, 4) electrodes in light (b) Photocurrent density–time curves of bare CdWO4 electrode and N-GQDs/CdWO4-1(2, 3, 4) electrodes.

(a) UV–visible DRS spectra of different samples; (b) energy dependence of (ahν)2; (c) Normalized changes in the MO concentration (C/C0) and (d) linear plots of -ln(C/C0) for the photodegradation of MO under UV light in the presence of different photocatalysts.
Cycling runs in the photodegradation of MO in the presence of N-GQDs-CdWO4-2.
Schematic presentation of the photodegradation process.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of N doped graphene quantum dots-interspersed CdWO4 heterostructure nanorods as an effective photocatalyst with enhanced photoelectrochemical performance

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Special Materials and Technology, Fudan University, Shanghai, 200433, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Special Materials and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and NanoEngineering, Rice University, Houston, 77005, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and NanoEngineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, N doped graphene quantum dots-interspersed Cadmium tungstate (CdWO4) nanorods (N-GQDs-CdWO4) were synthesized via decorating the N-GQDs on the surface of CdWO4 nanorods in three steps. The morphological and structural properties of the N-GQDs-CdWO4 nanorods were thoroughly investigated by X-ray diffraction spectroscopy, field-emission scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, and ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy. Based on imaging techniques, it became clear that the N-GQDs decorated on the surface of CdWO4 nanorods formed a heterostructure. This nanocomposite exhibits enhanced photoelectrochemical performances—more than four times those of bare CdWO4. In addition, the nanocomposite's photocatalytic activity under ultra-violet light irradiation was almost six fold that of bare CdWO4. We studied the mechanism governing its exceptional performance. It indicates that the existence of a heterostructure induces a synergistic effect between the N-GQDs and CdWO4 nanorods that bolsters photoelectric performance. Our work provides a feasible method to synthesize 0D/3D nanocomposites with enhanced photoelectrochemical performances, and offers a solution to rectify current solar energy shortfalls.

ce:section.ce:para: In an era where fossil fuels are being readily depleted and there is a dire need for alternative energy sources, researchers have turned their attention to solar power. Photo-electro-chemicals (PECs) harness the power of sunlight and can be employed in solar cells. This makes them an apt means to address global energy and environmental concerns [1,2]. TiO2 is a typical photoelectrochemical material that was discovered to be a photocatalyst in the 1970s [3]. It has been used for a large variety of applications due to the fact that it is abundant, nontoxic, and stable under photochemical conditions. Applications of TiO2 include hydrogen production for energy storage, as well as hydroxyl radical formation for pollutant degradation [4,5]. However, the large bandgap of TiO2 restricts its photoactivity in the ultraviolet (UV) region. This fact impedes its photoelectrochemical efficiency by diminishing reaction rates, and consequently precludes full solar energy utilization. Exploring alternative low-cost cocatalysts to replace traditional materials, like TiO2, is therefore an apposite pursuit [6,7].

Recently, tungstates have been widely investigated for their use as photocatalysts. For example, Bi2WO6 and ZnWO4 were confirmed to be efficient photocatalysts with enhanced photoactivities [8,9]. Among the various photocatalysts of tungstate systems, cadmium tungstate (CdWO4) with a monoclinic wolframite structure is considered to be a potential functional material. It has high average refractive index, marginal radiation damage potential, and high X-ray absorption coefficient [10].

Some works have reported that the photocatalytic activity of CdWO4 is equivalent to ZnWO4 and TiO2 under UV light illumination [11]. In another study, Xu et al. loaded CdWO4 nanorods onto graphene sheets, which enhanced their photocatalytic efficiency [12]. Unfortunately, bare CdWO4 has large band gap that circumscribes its photoelectrochemical efficiency. To date, many strategies have been proposed to resolve the synergistic issues of coupling CdWO4 with other materials to enhance photoelectrochemical performances [13–15]. Cao et al. fabricated a novel heterojunction photocatalyst CdWO4/BiOBr with enhanced photocatalytic performance [16].

Graphene-based materials possess remarkable photoelectrochemical performances and consequently have been employed in many fields, like energy storage [17–19]. Tayyebi and coworkers decorated zinc oxide quantum dots (QDs) onto graphene oxide (GO) surface via simple chemical method to promote the photocatalytic activity [20]. In particular, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) exhibit pronounced edge effects and unique phenomena that are rare in most types of semiconductors [21]. GQDs (less than 10 nm in size) are not very cytotoxic, are highly soluble, have stable photoluminescence, good biocompatibility, high specific surface area, high mobility, and tunable band gap, rendering them promising candidates for applications in photovoltaic devices, biosensing and imaging [18,22]. GQDs can be prepared in a variety of ways, Zheng et al. reported a microwave-assisted synthesis of high-quality nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) at room temperature and atmospheric pressure [23]. There have been few reports on the application of GQDs in the field of photocatalysis; only GQDs/TiO2, GQDs/Cu2O and GQDs/Fe2O3 have been studied [24–27]. Tian et al. synthesized N, S co-doped graphene quantum dots (N, S-GQDs) -reduced graphene oxide-(rGO)-TiO2 nanotubes (TiO2NT) composites as high performance photocatalysts [28]. Among these composites, GQDs are key components for ameliorating photocatalytic performance. Accordingly, we propose that the synergistic combination of CdWO4 with GQDs will facilitate better photoelectrochemical performance.

Herein, N-GQDs-interspersed CdWO4 nanorods were synthesized via a hydrothermal method. The CdWO4 nanorods serve as substrates that N-GQDs grow on. We demonstrate that N-GQDs and CdWO4 nanorods, when coupled in such a manner, form a heterostructure that enhances photocatalytic activity. The resulting heterostructure exhibited higher photocurrent and lower onset potential than bare CdWO4 nanorods. We then investigated the mechanisms governing their electrochemical performances. Different N-GQDs-interspersed CdWO4 nanorods were obtained by tuning the amount of N-GQDs. Ultimately, our investigation establishes a simple method to synthesize efficient photocatalyst and suggests a feasible route to design heterostructure composites.

Sodium nitrate (NaNO3), concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), 30% hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF), methyl orange, ethanol, cadmium diacetate dihydrate (Cd(OAc)2·2H2O), and sodium tungstate dihydrate (Na2WO4·2H2O) were of analytical grade, supplied by Sinopharm chemical reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China) and used without further purification. Graphite powder was supplied by Qingdao Baichuan graphite Co., Ltd (Qingdao, China). Deionized (DI) water was applied in all experiments.

Graphite oxide (GO) was synthesized from graphite powder by a modified Hummers' method [29,30]. 0.5 g NaNO3 and 23 mL concentrated H2SO4 were added into a 250 mL three-necked flask and then the mixture was stirred and cooled to 0 °C. When NaNO3 dissolved completely, 1 g graphite powder was added to the mixture. Then, we slowly put in 3 g KMnO4 to keep the temperature below 20 °C. Subsequently, the reaction mixture was heated to 35 °C and stirred for 1 h, after which 46 mL DI water was carefully added, producing a large amount of heat. External heating was also introduced to keep the reaction temperature at 98 °C for 15 min. Additional water (170 mL) and 30% H2O2 (5 mL) were mixed in afterwards. When it cooled to room temperature, the mixture was purified through multiple instances of centrifuging, decanting, and washing. The final product was obtained by vacuum drying at 45 °C overnight.

N-GQDs were prepared via a solvothermal method [31]. In a typical preparation process, 50 mg GO was dissolved in 20 mL DMF and subjected to sonication for 30 min. Then the dispersion was decanted into a 100 mL Teflon lined stainless autoclave and incubated in an oven at 200 °C for 6 h. After the solution cooled naturally, the as-obtained suspension was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to remove the black precipitates; a brown-yellow solution containing N-GQDs was thus acquired. The final products were collected after removing the solvent with the aid of a rotary evaporator.

The concentration of graphene quantum dots aqueous solution is 0.1 mg mL−1, 0.44 g Cd(OAc)2·2H2O was dissolved into 5 mL, 10 mL, 20 mL and 40 mL of N-GQDs aqueous solution, respectively. The solution was under vigorous stirring for 2 h. Then 25 mL aqueous solution containing 0.66 g Na2WO4·2H2O was added to the above-mentioned precursor solution and maintained constant stirring. The mixture was successively transferred into a 100 mL Teflon lined stainless autoclave and left at 160 °C for 24 h. After it naturally cooled to room temperature, the final solids were collected via filtration, washed with ultrapure water 3 times, and then dried in vacuum oven at 60 °C overnight. Different N-GQDs-CdWO4 nanocomposites were obtained by tuning the amount of N-GQDs solution. They are titled N-GQDs-CdWO4-1 to N-GQDs-CdWO4-4. Pristine CdWO4 samples were prepared by replacing the N-GQDs solution with DI water.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded with an X-ray diffractometer (Bruker D8 Advance) with a scanning rate of 5° min−1 in the 2θ range of 10°–90°. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images were acquired with a JEOL JEM-2100 LaB6 transmission electron microscope. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and elemental mapping images were obtained with a Tescan MAIA3 scanning electron microscope. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were attained via a Nicolet Is10 spectrometer. Raman spectra were recorded on a Raman microspectrometer (spex/403). X-ray photoelectron spectrometry (XPS) analyses were carried out on a PHI 5000C ESCA system. Lastly, room temperature UV–Vis absorption and UV–Vis diffusive reflectance spectra were captured using a Shimadzu UV-3600 spectrophotometer.

Photoelectrochemical investigation was conducted with a standard three-electrode system on an electrochemical workstation (Autolab PG 302N). The preparation process of working electrodes is as follows: production sample (0.5 g) was grounded with 0.1 g polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) and 1 mL alcohol, then stirred for 3 h. The slurry was deposited on an indium tin oxide (ITO) glass, and the active area was controlled to be 1.0 cm2. A Pt wire as the counter electrode and mercurous sulfate electrode as the reference electrode. Na2SO4 aqueous solution (1 mol L−1) was used as the supporting electrolyte.

The photocatalytic activities of the N-GQDs-CdWO4 nanocomposites were evaluated by photocatalytic degradation of MO in aqueous solution. In a typical experiment, 50 mg of photocatalyst was added to 100 mL MO solution (10 mg L−1). Prior to irradiation, the suspensions were stirred in the dark for 1 h to ensure adsorption/desorption equilibrium between the catalysts and organic pollutant. 3 mL aliquots were sampled at 15-min time intervals and analyzed on a UV–Vis spectrophotometer to test the concentrations of the organic contaminant. The aliquots were centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 5 min to remove the photocatalysts powders before spectroscopy measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterizations of N-GQDs/CdWO4 nanorods
ce:section.ce:para: First, N-GQDs were obtained via a solvothermal method. Then, we prepared CdWO4 nanorods attached to N-GQDs with a hydrothermal method, as shown in Fig. 1 a. More details about the synthesis process can be found in the Experimental Section. The crystal phases of the bare CdWO4 and N-GQDs/CdWO4 nanorods were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD). As illustrated in Fig. 1b, the pattern of bare CdWO4 ranges from 10° to 70°. The characteristic diffraction peaks can all be attributed to the wolframite of monoclinic CdWO4 phase, according to the standcard JCPDS 14-0676. Notably, the diffraction peaks are distinct, indicating that both the bare CdWO4 and the nanocomposite were fully crystallized [10]. Furthermore, the peaks of bare CdWO4 and the peaks of the nanocomposite closely align, meaning that complexation of CdWO4 and graphene quantum dots has essentially no effect on the phase composition and crystallinity of CdWO4 nanorods. The diffraction peaks at 17.85°, 23.27°, 28.99°, 29.55°, 30.50°, 35.36°, 35.68°, 39.36°, 39.98°, 42.38°, 43.21°, 47.41°, 50.40°, 51.84°, 60.72°, 63.10°, and 66.38° correspond to the (100), (110), (−111), (111), (020), (002), (200), (−102), (121), (−112), (112), (022), (−202), (202), (311), (132), and (041) crystal planes of CdWO4 nanorods.

The morphologies of the composites were investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). From SEM images, as shown in Fig. 2 a and b, the shape of cadmium tungate is rod-like. The average width is about 500 nm. Also, there is a small amount of agglomerated CdWO4 particles around the nanorods.

Energy Dispersive Spectrometer (EDS) analyses were employed to further verify whether or not the composite was successfully synthesized. Elemental mapping images (Fig. 2c, d, e, f, g) pinpoint major chemical components as C, N, O, Cd and W, uniformly distributed across the surface of the sample. The presence of O, Cd, and W elements indicate the formation of CdWO4, whereas the C, N and a portion of the O elements can be attributed to N-GQDs. Moreover, the calculated element ratio of C: O: Cd: W is approximately 3:5:1:1, which stoichiometrically validates the fabrication of N-GQDs interspersed on the surface of CdWO4 nanorods.

In the TEM images of N-GQDs/CdWO4 nanorods (Fig. 2h and i) highlight the crystal lattice of CdWO4. The lattice's interplanar spacing is 0.494 nm, which is consistent with the (100) crystal plane of monoclinic CdWO4 (JCPDS card no. 14-0676) [32]. Moreover, it displayed that there existed two different lattices in the edge area. By calculating the lattice spacing, it suggests that the N-GQDs were decorated on the surface of CdWO4. Lastly, the resolution TEM images of N-GQDs/CdWO4 nanorods demonstrate that the Graphene quantum dots are seamlessly bonded to the CdWO4 surface along its edges.

The elemental components of the N-GQDs/CdWO4 nanorods were ascertained again with X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements. Fig. 3 a shows a typical XPS profile for the N-GQDs/CdWO4 nanorods. The XPS figure verifies that the nanocomposite is absolutely composed of five elements: C, N, O, Cd and W. No other elemental peak signals were detected by the XPS spectrum. The peaks at 286 eV and 397 eV are contributions from the N-GQDs and correspond to carbon (C 1s) and nitrogen (N 1s). In addition, a W 4f7 peak at 36 eV, two W 4d5 peaks-one at 261 eV and the other at 276 eV-a characteristic Cd 3d5/2 peak at 406 eV, and a Cd 3d3/2 peak at 413 eV, indicate the formation of CdWO4. On the other hand, the peak at 532 eV signifies the existence of oxygen (O 1s) and can therefore be attributed to both the N-GQDs and CdWO4. A high resolution scan of the C 1s region is displayed in Fig. 3b. Here, the peak that is located at 283.8 eV corresponds to the sp2 C in the N-GQDs, namely CC and CC bonds [33].

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was conducted to compare the chemical structures of GQDs, bare CdWO4, and N-GQDs/CdWO4 nanorods. As shown in Fig. S1, the spectrum of the GQDs has a broad absorption band at 3000-3500 cm−1. The absorption band value is a factor of the stretching vibrations of OH and NH bonds and suggests that there are many hydroxyl and amino groups on the surface of the N-GQDs. The bands at 1618 cm−1, 1400 cm−1, and 1138 cm−1 are results of the chemical groups like -CO-NR2, CN and epoxy/ether [31].

For bare CdWO4, there is a sharp absorption peak at 3467 cm−1 due to the H2O adsorbed on its surface. Otherwise, peaks between 500 cm−1-1000 cm−1 are indicative of the CdWO4 itself; at 818 cm−1, there is evidence for the asymmetrical stretching vibrations of the WOW bond. Lastly, the peak at 580 cm−1 corresponds to the in-plane deformation of WO4 2− groups [32,34]. These characteristic peaks in the FTIR spectra can also be found in spectrum of N-GQDs-CdWO4-2 composite and demonstrate the effective complexation of N-GQDs/CdWO4 nanorods.

The Raman spectra of CdWO4, N-GQDs and N-GQDs-CdWO4-2 are displayed in Fig. 4 . There are two main peaks at 1334 cm−1 and 1581 cm−1 for the N-GQDs, corresponding to the D-band and G-band of the N-GQDs. All characteristic bands (a strong peak at 895 cm−1 and weaker peaks at 227, 267, 304, 348, 385, 514, 544, 687 and 771 cm−1) of bare CdWO4 are detected in the Raman spectra. These peaks are characteristic of the monoclinic structure and agree with previous reports [12,35]. Weak peaks at 1324 cm−1 (D-band) and 1576 cm−1 (G-band) can also be identified in the Raman spectrum of N-GQDs-CdWO4-2 composite and show slight blue shift compared to the N-GQDs. This shift may be a consequence of charge transfer between the N-GQDs and CdWO4 [36,37].

The PEC activity of the bare CdWO4 and the N-GQDs/CdWO4 nanorods was characterized with and without UV-light illumination. The photoresponse performance of bare CdWO4 is shown in Fig. 5 by several means. Namely, photocurrent density–potential curves (I–V curve) in Fig. 5a obtained by linear sweep voltammetry indicate the strong enhancement of polarization current. The photocurrent in the bare CdWO4 electrode is about 3 times higher than that in dark with an obviously lower onset potential. Fig. 5b details the chopping photocurrent response test of bare CdWO4 electrode. It suggests a rapid, stable, and reproducible feature. When illuminated by UV-light, a current spike is witnessed immediately because the photoresponse for all the dark-light switches and the photocurrent quickly decays to a stable state.

In order to improve the PEC activity of bare CdWO4 nanocomposites, graphene quantum dots were introduced to synthesize a series of N-GQDs/CdWO4 (GQDs/CdWO4- 1, 2, 3, 4) nanocomposites with varying graphene quantum dot mass ratios. The PEC performance of the N-GQDs/CdWO4 nanocomposites was investigated by photocurrent response tests depicted in Fig. 6 . The corresponding EIS measurements of the electrodes are shown in Fig. 6a.

Since the four N-GQDs/CdWO4 electrodes have a smaller radius than that of bare CdWO4 electrode, the introduction of graphene decreases the charge-transfer resistance of composites. The radius of N-GQDs-CdWO4-2 was the smallest and follows this trend; it demonstrated the highest current density. Photocurrent density versus time curves of four N-GQDs/CdWO4 and bare CdWO4 electrodes are plotted in Fig. 6b. The photoresponse of four N-GQDs/CdWO4 electrodes is fast, steady, and reproducible like bare CdWO4 electrode. However, the photocurrent density of the N-GQDs-CdWO4-2 electrode is almost 3 times higher than that of the bare CdWO4 electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic and enhancement mechanism of N-GQDs/CdWO4 nanorods
ce:section.ce:para: UV–vis diffuse reflection spectroscopy conducted to investigate the optical properties of both CdWO4 and N-GQDs/CdWO4 nanorods. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the addition of N-GQDs heavily influenced the visual properties of CdWO4. Compared to bare CdWO4, N-GQDs/CdWO4 nanorods exhibit higher absorption intensity in the UV light region (mainly 200 nm–400 nm). Such can be attributed to the black body effect that the additional N-GQDs provide, disposing the structure to the absorption of UV light [12,38].

UV-light absorption capacity of different samples ranks as follow: N-GQDs-CdWO4-2 > N-GQDs-CdWO4-3 > N-GQDs-CdWO4-1 > N-GQDs-CdWO4-4. On basis of the Kubelka-Munk theory, the optical absorption band gap Eg of semiconductor can be calculated by the following equation: (ahν)n = A(hν - Eg), where a stands for the absorption coefficient, hv is the incident photon energy, n is the index depending on the type of the semiconductor, and A represents a proportionality constant relative to chosen materials [12,39]. For CdWO4, an indirect gap semiconductor, n equals 1/2. In Fig. 7b, the Eg was determined by extrapolation of the linear portion of the (ahν)1/2 curve versus the photon energy hν to (ahν)1/2 = 0. Moreover, it can be found that the band gap of pristine CdWO4 is 3.62 eV. After the addition of N-GQDs, the band gap of N-GQDs/CdWO4 nanocomposites changed to 3.54 eV, 3.21 eV, 3.70 eV, and 3.71 eV, corresponding to N-GQDs-CdWO4-1 through N-GQDs-CdWO4-4, respectively.

Semiconductors can produce electron-hole pairs under illumination with photon energy higher than their band gap. Those electrons and holes are able to transfer to the surface of the crystal, which makes semiconductors react with H2O and molecular oxygen. During the reaction between H2O and O2, hydroxyl radicals and superoxide radicals are generated, which are highly reactive and oxidative [40,41]. As such, they can degrade the harmful organic materials into CO2, H2O, NO3-, etc.

In order to determine the visible light photocatalytic performance of the N-GQDs/CdWO4 nanorods, the degradation of methyl orange (MO) was studied. In darkness, no significant degradation of the MO solution was observed after 1 h in the presence of our nanorods photocatalysts. Fig. 7c juxtaposes the photocatalytic performance of CdWO4 and N-GQDs-CdWO4 nanocomposites. Clearly N-GQDs-CdWO4-2 exhibits better photocatalytic properties. According to the Lambert-Beer law, d
e
% (degradation efficiency) is calculated by: d
e
% = (C
0
-C
f
)/C
0 × 100% = (A
0
-A
f
)/A
0 × 100%, where C
0 and C
f represent the initial and final MO concentrations, and A
0 and A
f represent the initial and final absorbances [42,43]. Applying this formula, under UV light illumination for 3 h, the degradation efficiencies of bare CdWO4 were calculated to be 9.3%. In contrast, degradation efficiencies of N-GQDs-CdWO4 nanocomposites show remarkable increases of 20.5%, 54.7%, 38.8%, and 25.1%. In addition, after 6 h illumination, N-GQDs-CdWO4-2 demonstrated outstanding degradation efficiency of 93.8%, significantly improved compared to the 14.9% degradation efficiency of pristine CdWO4.

Photocatalytic degradation reactions are usually assumed to abide by the pseudo-first-order expression -ln(C
0
/C) = kt, where C/C
0 represents the normalized MO concentration and k stands for the apparent reaction rate (min−1). By plotting -ln(C
0
/C) as a function of irradiation time through linear regression, the observed rate constant k, which is an important index to evaluate photoactivity, can be obtained from the slopes of the simulated straight lines [44,45]. Fig. 7d highlights the relationship between -ln(C
0
/C) and the irradiation time (t) for the photocatalytic degradation of MO under UV light. The reaction rate constant for the bare CdWO4 is 4.01 × 10−4 min−1, but for N-GQD-CdWO4 nanocomposites, it increases to 1.29 × 10−3 min−1, 6.82 × 10−3 min−1, 2.74 × 10−3 min−1, and 1.53 × 10−3 min−1, respectively. The increase in rate constant confirms that the addition of N-GQDs enhanced the photoactivity of CdWO4 under UV light. Moreover, we have investigated the photostability of N-GQDs-CdWO4-2 sample by repeated photocatalytic experiments. As shown in Fig. 8 , in the first three cycles of photocatalytic tests, N-GQDs-CdWO4-2 showed degradation efficiencies of 93.8%, 91.9%, and 89.8%, respectively. This result shows that N-GQDs-CdWO4-2 nanocomposite not only possesses much higher photocatalytic activity but also has good stability. This may be attributed to the synergetic effect between N-GQDs and CdWO4, since the photogenerated photons and electron could transfer from CdWO4 to N-GQDs, thus protecting CdWO4 from photocorrosion and improving its photostability.
Enhanced photoactivity can be ascribed to the synergetic effect between the N-GQDs and CdWO4. The proposed mechanism is shown in Fig. 9 .

In the first step, N-GQDs possess unique upconversion properties, which can absorb the light and then emit photon with shorter wavelength. The black body effect of the N-GQDs also promotes absorption of UV light, expanding the light absorption range of N-GQDs-CdWO4 nanocomposite compared to pristine CdWO4. As the excitation of quantum dots is mainly in the UV region and the CdWO4 is an indirect gap semiconductor with band gap Eg of 3.9 eV, so the increased absorption of light is mainly concentrated in the UV region. Then, when subjected to light illumination, the photo-induced electrons are more efficiently transfered between GQDs and CdWO4. Through this electron transfer process, N-GQDs may protect CdWO4 from photocorrosion and reinforce the photostability of N-GQDs-CdWO4 composites [46,47]. Furthermore, the N-GQDs also serve as an electron reservoir that traps electrons emitted from CdWO4. This facilitates charge transfer and separation, suppress the charge recombination of electron-hole pairs, and yields improved photocurrent response and photoactivity [48,49].

In summary, novel N-GQDs-CdWO4 nanorods with enhanced photoelectrochemical performances were successfully fabricated by a facile one-step method. The N-GQDs-CdWO4 nanocomposites exhibited markedly higher photocatalytic abilities and photodegradation efficiencies compared to pristine CdWO4. Their excellent performances can be attributed to the synergetic effect between N-GQDs and CdWO4. We thoroughly discussed the mechanism on the basis of photoelectrochemical measurement and photoactivity characterization. Namely, graphene quantum dots extract the photogenerated electrons from pristine CdWO4, which enhance charge transfer and separation and suppress the charge recombination of electron-hole pairs. Consequently, N-GQDs-CdWO4 nanocomposites have wider light response range, better photocurrent response, and more stable performance. The fabrication of this unique nanocomposite has not only established a new method to synthesize photocatalysts, but it also clears the path to full-scale implementation, reducing environmental pollution, and addressing energy issues.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jallcom.2017.05.237.
